coredata.dc:title: Converting fruit waste into carbon dots for bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, P.
Verma, G.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are 0-D nanomaterials with unique structural and optical properties. Compared to fluorescent organic agents or semiconductor quantum dots, CDs offer several advantages such as higher photostability, lower toxicity, better biocompatibility, easier synthesis, and surface modifications. This endows CDs with immense potential to serve many applications like photocatalysis, biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, memory devices, etc. Fruit waste is a perfect choice to synthesize CDs as it is compositionally rich, easily available, renewable, and sustainable. It also caters well to the ongoing research interests of the scientific community to use environmental waste for advanced technologies. Modern solutions for fruit waste handling are urgently needed as the conventional methods are just not sufficient to realize the full potential of treasured fruit parts, which are otherwise termed as ‘waste’. In this scenario, the present review article is dedicated to understanding the conversion of fruit waste into CDs for bioimaging applications. It discusses feasibilities and superiorities of adopting fruit waste as carbon source, merits and demerits of synthesis, bioimaging techniques, morphology, and optical properties of CDs. Some other major factors, which affect the fluorescence emission of CDs, such as dopants, chemical composition, hydrophilicity, surface-passivation, defects, etc., are analyzed too. The article concludes with a discussion of challenges and future perspectives of fruit waste-derived CDs for wider implications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass, Fluorescent dots, Surface modifications, Photoluminescence, Quantum yield, Cytotoxicity, Biocompatibility, Biomedical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are 0-D nanomaterials with unique structural and optical properties. Compared to fluorescent organic agents or semiconductor quantum dots, CDs offer several advantages such as higher photostability, lower toxicity, better biocompatibility, easier synthesis, and surface modifications. This endows CDs with immense potential to serve many applications like photocatalysis, biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, memory devices, etc. Fruit waste is a perfect choice to synthesize CDs as it is compositionally rich, easily available, renewable, and sustainable. It also caters well to the ongoing research interests of the scientific community to use environmental waste for advanced technologies. Modern solutions for fruit waste handling are urgently needed as the conventional methods are just not sufficient to realize the full potential of treasured fruit parts, which are otherwise termed as ‘waste’. In this scenario, the present review article is dedicated to understanding the conversion of fruit waste into CDs for bioimaging applications. It discusses feasibilities and superiorities of adopting fruit waste as carbon source, merits and demerits of synthesis, bioimaging techniques, morphology, and optical properties of CDs. Some other major factors, which affect the fluorescence emission of CDs, such as dopants, chemical composition, hydrophilicity, surface-passivation, defects, etc., are analyzed too. The article concludes with a discussion of challenges and future perspectives of fruit waste-derived CDs for wider implications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Biomass, Fluorescent dots, Surface modifications, Photoluminescence, Quantum yield, Cytotoxicity, Biocompatibility, Biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Food is most important to us for sustainable living. With a continual increase in the world population, both the production and consumption of food are growing too. As per the UNEP index report (2021), ∼931 million tonnes of food waste are getting generated every year from the food industry, retail establishments, and household levels [1]. Food waste is the third-largest source of greenhouse gas emissions (∼4.4 Gt CO2 eq. yr−1), and only fruits generate ∼45% of the total waste [2]. The conventional fruit waste management technologies (animal feeding, landfilling, anaerobic digestion, composting, combustion, or gasification) may be advantageous as partial substitutes to the hazardous chemical technologies, but many serious drawbacks are also associated with them. The thermal techniques (combustion or gasification) demand higher operational and investment costs. Landfilling is the least desired approach as it causes serious environmental issues, while others (anaerobic digestion, composting, or animal feeding) generate low-value products [3]. The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using fruit waste is a new approach to handling environmental waste while fostering the advanced applications [4]. Fruit waste is an underutilized source enriched with carbohydrates, proteins, minerals, and several bioactive compounds [5]. It is much more beneficial to use fruit waste as the carbon source to synthesize CDs than the chemically driven or commercially available carbon sources. It is an economical and environmentally benign source that is readily available, inexhaustible, and bountiful [6,7].

In 2004, CDs were discovered by Scrivens et al. while purification of the single-wall carbon nanotubes [8]. In 2006, Sun et al. named these fluorescent carbon nanomaterials as ‘carbon dots’ [9]. CDs are the 0-D nanomaterials with lateral size ranges <10 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm corresponding to the (002) and (100) graphitic planes, respectively. As compared to the traditional quantum dots, CDs exhibit higher photostability, dispersion rate, wide emission, and narrow half-width characteristics. Typically, CDs have a core-shell structure, which consists of a carbon core and the oxygen/nitrogen functionalities connected to its surface. The core can be crystalline, semi-crystalline, or amorphous. The CDs are synthesized following top-down or bottom-up approaches [10]. A myriad of carbon sources exists to synthesize CDs with versatile features. To broaden the application potential of CDs, it is further possible to modulate their surface with functional groups or introduce different heteroatom dopants, e.g., nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, phosphorous, boron, etc. Although much is explored for the synthesis of CDs, there are no set principles to synthesize CDs with the desired control over their optical features. The structure-property relationships related to CDs are not clear till date [11].

Photoluminescence (PL) is one of the most interesting properties of CDs. The widely accepted mechanisms for the PL in CDs are the surface states, carbon core states, and combined effects of these two. It is anticipated that the PL is tuned with the surface functional groups and particle size of CDs. Wang et al. reported that the selection of reaction precursors, control of synthesis conditions, and purification process are the key parameters to achieve high quantum yield (QY) and multicolor emission in CDs [12]. However, it is a great challenge to control the synthesis conditions to get the desired PL in CDs. Even a small change in reaction precursors, before or after processing treatments, can give completely different optical properties, which makes CDs a very complicated system to understand. It is a major task to achieve full-color emission with high fluorescence (FL) intensity in CDs. It is also difficult to achieve long-lasting PL from the CDs. A novel research direction is to develop afterglow emission CDs; however, as the field is new, it needs explorations for its successful implementations in the future [13]. He et al. suggested the need of in-depth photo-physical characterization studies (femtosecond transient absorption, in-situ PL, time-resolved electron paramagnetic resonance, scanning tunneling microscopy, steady state-transient state PL measurements, etc.) of CDs in order to understand the PL origin mechanisms [14].

CDs have shown tremendous potential for applications in the biomedical field. Compared to the existing bioprobes, CDs exhibit prominent biocompatibility, higher brightness, lower toxicity, superior photostability, better penetration capabilities, and hydrophilicity [15]. The bioimaging potential of CDs has been discussed in some excellent review articles recently [16–18]. Anwar et al. discussed the structural and optical features of CDs with emphasis on deep-red to near-infrared (NIR) emissive CDs, two-photon FL, phosphorescence, and functionalized CDs for applications in bioimaging, photodynamic therapy, photothermal therapy, and antibacterial agents [16]. Li et al. presented a comprehensive survey of research in the past three years for the bioimaging applications of CDs. It discussed the in vitro and in vivo bioimaging of different types of cells (cancer, stem, and neural cells), cell organelles (nucleus, mitochondria, and lysosomes), tumor therapeutics, as well as image-guided drug delivery [17]. Unnikrishnan et al. discussed the types and properties of CDs for the selective labeling with subcellular organelles e.g., lysosomes, nucleus, nucleolus, mitochondrion, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, and others [18].

So far, extensive studies have been reported on the blue- and green-emission CDs as they are easier to synthesize. But there are many limitations to their use for bioimaging as they exhibit intense blue autofluorescence in tissues, low-penetration depth for short wavelength photons, photodamaging, and strong tissue absorption. So, a recent interest is to design CDs with longer wavelength emissions confined in the biological transparency window (650–1350 nm) [19]. The advantages of red-emission CDs are low photodamage to biological tissues, deeper tissue penetration, and lesser autofluorescence interferences. Younis et al. discussed optical imaging modalities and their advantages for bioimaging [10]. The optical properties of red-emission CDs depend on their size, chemical structure, and crystallinity. To date, it is possible to achieve up to 100% QY of green- and blue-emission CDs but the QY of red-emission CDs is limited. It is due to the poor conjugation between reaction precursors and synthesis conditions for these CDs. So, it needs to understand better the surface chemistry and exact PL mechanisms for the practical applicability of red-emission CDs [20].

In 2012, with the first-ever reporting on the synthesis of CDs using grass, the research was triggered globally to use natural materials for synthesizing CDs [21]. Biomass is advantageous as it contains many organic constituents to impart self-passivation and hydrophilicity to CDs. Also, the synthesis of CDs is relatively economical and facile compared to chemicals-based CDs. Biomass from miscellaneous categories, e.g., fruits and vegetables, food and beverages, animal and human derivatives, plants and their parts, industrial waste, etc., is used to synthesize CDs for multifarious applications [22]. However, the desired success could not be achieved till date as precise control of the size, optical properties, and surface groups of CDs is difficult to attain. Still, it remains to explore the large-scale synthesis, morphology, PL, surface chemistry, long-wavelength emission, and synthesis mechanisms of biomass-derived CDs [23].

Although great efforts have been taken to convert biomass into CDs, it needs more focused studies on an individual class of materials to tackle the above-outlined issues. Natural materials are diverse in terms of their origin and choices. It also leads to diversity in their materialistic properties despite their belonging to the same family under broad classifications. So, to just summarize the biomass under one category of green precursors is not enough. This review article is written to address this gap. Fruit waste is particularly selected for the discussion as fruits are consumed daily by most living beings. Fruits may cause 10–70% of environmental waste as per their mode of consumption [24]. The review article discusses the conversion of fruit waste into CDs for bioimaging applications. Fig. 1 shows the overall process for converting fruit waste into CDs. A discussion of various important parameters, such as PL origin, fruit waste composition, synthesis methods, surface chemistry, bioimaging techniques, biocompatibility, hydrophilicity, defects, and surface states of CDs, is provided. The achievements and challenges related to fruit waste-derived CDs are discussed, and futuristic directions are enlightened from the application point of view.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs using fruit waste
ce:section.ce:para: Fruit waste-derived CDs are mainly synthesized via bottom-up approaches (thermal, combustion, or microwave heating). So, the focus of the discussion here will be on hydrothermal carbonization (HTC), pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted methods. In Table 1 , some properties and applications of CDs are enlisted, which are synthesized using different natural materials following bottom-up synthesis. A discussion on other top-down and bottom-up methods (e.g., template-based, laser ablation, ultrasonic, thermolysis, arc discharge, solvothermal, and electrochemical oxidation [25–27]) is appended in Section 5.

It is an environment-friendly technique to convert natural materials (organic waste, animal manure, agricultural residues, etc.) into value-added carbon materials [28]. It undergoes at high temperature and pressure for a few to several hours. The HTC method was pioneered by Bergius in 1913 [29]. He reported the hydrothermal conversion of cellulose into coal-like materials. The methodology was gradually accepted by the scientific community. Based on the experimental conditions, HTC is of two types, i.e., high-temperature HTC (300–800 °C) and low-temperature HTC (150–250 °C). High-temperature HTC is used to synthesize graphite, carbon nanotubes, or activated carbons; however, it is beyond the stability of some standard organic compounds [30]. Low-temperature HTC is an environment-friendly, spontaneous and benign approach with carbon efficiency ∼1 [31]. It is the same as the natural coalification process, but it exhibits accelerated speed and lesser reaction time. During the HTC process, the synthesis of CDs takes place via four stages that are dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [32]. A step-by-step methodology for the HTC synthesis of CDs using glycine is shown in Fig. 2 a. Initially, the molecules in glycine assemble via hydrogen bonding. Upon heating, the dehydration and polymerization reactions take place, giving rise to a short burst of nucleation, and these nuclei further grow into CDs.

Various fruit peels have been processed via the HTC approach to synthesize CDs. The orange peels were given the HTC treatment to synthesize CDs, as shown in Fig. 2b [33]. Surendran et al. treated the mixture of orange peel waste and citric acid to synthesize CDs following the HTC method [34]. Hydrophilic N-doped CDs were synthesized using dwarf banana peels with HTC treatment (Fig. 2c) [35]. Another study reported HTC conversion of banana peels into CDs [36]. Nguyen et al. processed the banana peels hydrothermally to synthesize activated carbons and CDs [37]. Su et al. treated dried lemon peels via the HTC method to synthesize CDs [38]. The grapefruit peels were hydrothermally converted into CDs [39]. The mixture of pineapple peels juice and ethanol were given the HTC treatment to synthesize luminescent CDs [40]. Lu et al. treated pomelo peels via HTC methodology to synthesize CDs [41]. Atchudan et al. reported the hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CDs using kiwi fruit peels [42].

HTC is an advantageous methodology to convert fruit waste into CDs. It is a simpler approach that uses compact design reactors, and the recovery of final products from reactants is also easy. It avoids complicated drying and isolation mechanisms too [43,44]. However, the quality of the final product is controlled by the temperature, pressure, reaction time, catalysts, and biomass to water ratio, and so it demands careful observance of the operating conditions. A major problem with the HTC process is the generation of a large amount of wastewater and liquid oil as by-products, which needs to be addressed.

It is the thermochemical conversion of biomass into carbonaceous architectures with desired properties [45]. Biomass consists of three biopolymers that are lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose. Various reactions that occur during the pyrolysis process are dehydration, depolymerization, isomerization, aromatization, decarboxylation, and charring. Overall, the process undergoes three mechanisms that are moisture evaporation, breakage, and formation of chemical bonds as well as polymerization [46,47]. The key parameters to control the properties of the final product are temperature, reaction time, heating rate, choice of natural materials, and reaction environment. The product quality upon pyrolysis may be diverse depending upon the type and origin of the raw precursors. The same material pyrolyzed under different reaction conditions may give rise to completely different properties in the final product. Pyrolysis is a widely accepted methodology to carbonize biomass as it recovers much more yield from the reactants [48]. However, it requires high operational costs and some means of discarding the incondensable gases and toxic ash content that may cause environmental issues. Krysmann et al. reported the step-by-step pyrolysis mechanism to synthesize CDs from citric acid and ethanolamine [49]. All the reaction steps and PL spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 2d. After 30 min of pyrolysis, amide groups were formed due to intermolecular condensation reactions and moisture evaporation. With increase in the temperature, spherical nanoparticles were produced because of the crosslinking reactions and interchain imide bond formations. At 300 °C, the CDs were formed. With further heating, the aggregates of CDs were produced. It gave rise to non-uniform and large-sized carbon particles. The PL of CDs was due to the amide-group fluorophores.

Different fruit peels and seeds have been processed via pyrolysis to synthesize CDs. The citrus fruit peels (orange and lemon) were pyrolyzed to produce CDs [50]. Zhou et al. followed a low-temperature carbonization approach to synthesize CDs using watermelon peels [51]. Xue et al. reported pyrolysis of lychee exocarp waste to synthesize CDs. The CDs were also surface-passivated with polyethylene glycol (PEG) diamine [52]. Aji et al. processed mangosteen fruit peels to synthesize CDs via pyrolysis [53]. Durian peels wastes were pyrolyzed to synthesize CDs [54]. Chatzimarkou et al. pyrolyzed apple seeds to achieve nitrogen-doped CDs [55]. Lychee seeds were also given the pyrolysis treatment to synthesize CDs [56].

It is one of the highly cost-effective approaches to producing CDs. The CDs are mostly synthesized with a high product yield following this methodology. Putro et al. processed the cassava peels via the microwave-assisted method to synthesize CDs [57]. The complete synthesis methodology is shown in Fig. 2e. The sample was irradiated in a microwave oven for 40 min to synthesize CDs. This method is advantageous as compared to the convection or conduction-based heating methods. However, some incomplete reactions may take a longer time to finish because of the heterogeneous temperature distributions in the sample and reaction vessel. The intermediate reactions give rise to unwanted by-products, and a mechanistic understanding of such processes is still lacking. So furthermore, clarifications on the final products and reaction intermediates are desired.

Gudimella et al. treated orange peels waste via a novel sand bath-assisted method to synthesize CDs [58]. It used uniformly sieved sand to maintain a constant temperature during the synthesis process. With this methodology, biomolecules (cellulose, phenol groups, flavonoids, proteins, etc.) in orange peels were transformed into CDs via carbonization and dehydration reactions. Interestingly, this method neither involved acidic or basic solutions nor surface-passivating agents. However, it needs futuristic investigations for its wider implications.

Bioimaging is the visualization of functional or structural images of biological systems in real-time without actually making a physical interference. Bioimaging techniques are the instruments or methods to acquire the images at desired temporal and spatial scales. The optical modalities used for bioimaging are FL, ultrasound, electrons, X-ray, light, positrons, and magnetic resonance [10]. In recent times, FL imaging has received substantial attention. Several FL bioprobes exist for the bioimaging like organic dyes, metal nanoclusters, quantum dots, and core-shell nanoparticles. However, as they contain heavy toxic elements, their biomedical applications are not safe. For cellular imaging, biocompatible probes with low toxicity are needed. The CDs fit well in this frame as they exhibit excellent PL, lower toxicity, and higher biocompatibility. In this section, one-photon and two-photon FL techniques for cellular imaging, using CDs, are discussed.

It is one of the widely used and efficient methods for cellular imaging. For the FL bioimaging, light is irradiated from the NIR region as this radiation can easily penetrate through the human tissues. Various benefits of this technique include high sensitivity, good spatial resolution, high-performance capability, and low cost. Several precursors of diverse origins have been explored by different research groups to prepare CDs for the in vitro and in vivo bioimaging with FL microscopy. Zhang et al. prepared bee pollen of rapeseed flower-derived CDs conjugates for imaging of LoVo cells [59]. Qin et al. tested the biocompatibility of black fungus-derived CDs toward HeLa cells [60]. The emission and excitation wavelengths were 450 and 370 nm, respectively. Bandi et al. synthesized cigarette buts-derived CDs and applied them for bioimaging of normal and cancerous human cells [61]. Li et al. prepared ginkgo leaves-based nitrogen and sulfur-doped CDs and incubated them with KYSE410 and HeLa cells for the in vitro cellular imaging [62]. Lignin (waste from paper and pulp industry)-derived CDs were incubated with HeLa cells using WST-8 colorimetric assay [63]. Manilkara zapota fruit-derived CDs were employed for in vitro bioimaging using E. coli, Aspergillus aculeatus, and Fomitopsis sp. cell models [64]. The cytotoxicity of CDs was tested on HeLa cells using the MTT assay. Another study tested the biocompatibility of silkworm chrysalises-derived CDs for cellular imaging of HeLa cells [65]. The FL images were obtained using an inverted FL microscope. Ding et al. reported the FL imaging of lemon juice-derived CDs toward HeLa cells using the MTT assay [66].

In 2009, Sun’s group was the first to use CDs for the in vivo optical imaging of live mice [67]. The potential of CDs is widely explored afterward for real-time applications. Atchudan et al. performed the in vivo cellular imaging of C. elegans (nematode) after staining them with Indian gooseberry fruit-derived CDs [68]. The CDs exhibited good biocompatibility, the near absence of toxicity and higher cell viability. In another study, CDs were synthesized using wheat straw and then employed for optical imaging of mice inoculated with Smmc-7721 tumor cells [69]. The phenolic extracts of coffee bean shells were processed to synthesize CDs [70]. The optical imaging of a tumor in a nude mouse was performed by inducing CDs-stained HeLa cells in it. The images revealed selective uptake of CDs in the liver and tumor site of the mouse and not in the other organs such as the kidney, lung, or heart.

Although FL imaging has been widely accepted for bioimaging applications, it is observed that the cell incubation with CDs emits FL mainly due to the accumulation of CDs in the cells. The bare CDs are majorly seen in the cytoplasmic region and the cell membrane. So, extensive efforts are ongoing to surface modify the CDs with peptides, antibodies, and more biomolecules of interest for targeted bioimaging. Such modifications of CDs enhance their specific grafting to the overexpressed biomarkers or reprehensive receptor regions. Novel NIR receptive CDs are also being designed with the longer emission and excitation wavelengths in order to resolve the deep tissue penetration issues.

This technique allows cellular imaging of living tissues up to ∼1 mm in thickness along with 3.35 μm axial and 0.64 μm lateral spatial resolution. Unlike traditional one-photon FL imaging, in which, the emission wavelength is longer than the excitation wavelength, two-photon FL imaging needs simultaneous excitation with two photons having a longer wavelength than the emission light. It typically needs a NIR region excitation of light. Two-photon excitation serves two purposes, i.e., infrared radiations minimize light scattering in the tissue, and the background signal is strongly suppressed owing to multiphoton absorption. So, deep tissue penetration is possible with this technique. Two-photon FL imaging is superior to one-photon imaging in terms of efficient light detection, reduced photobleaching, and deeper tissue penetration.

For the first time, the potential of two-photon FL microscopy in imaging was demonstrated by Cao et al. in 2007 [71]. It has been confirmed that the absorption cross-section of CDs is equivalent to the CdSe–ZnS quantum dots. Kuo et al. reported that the surface modification of graphene quantum dots with nitrogen and amino functional groups is favorable for two-photon imaging in 3-D biological environments [72]. The modified graphene quantum dots exhibited higher stability under two-photon excitation and an absolute cross-section of ∼54356 Goeppert-Mayer units. Zhao et al. incubated CDs with HeLa cells for two-photon FL imaging, which revealed their selective accumulation in lysosomes [73]. Nitrogen-doped CDs were employed for the real-time monitoring of endocytosis and intracellular detection [74]. The CDs performed βG (β-glucuronidase) sensing in human serum with a good quality two-photon FL imaging. Song et al. employed peptide-conjugated CDs as FL bioprobes for the two-photon live-cell imaging of the B16–F10 cells [75]. Li et al. designed watermelon-derived CDs for the two-photon FL imaging of mice using an MTT assay [76]. Liu et al. prepared highly efficient red-emissive CDs and incubated them with HeLa cell tumor spheroids [77]. The cellular models were imaged under the confocal and two-photon optical modalities. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the imaging depth of the tumor exceeded 200 μm easily for two-photon excitation; however, a weaker luminescence intensity of tumor spheroid was observed under single-photon excitation. Fig. 3b shows two-photon FL imaging of the 96 hpf zebrafish larvae diluted in an aqueous solution of CDs. Under two-photon excitation mode, a maximum penetration depth of ∼500 μm was achieved; however, only the near side of the fish larvae was imaged under the confocal excitation mode.

Two-photon FL imaging is advantageous as it allows deeper penetration, higher resolution, low cell damage, low photobleaching, and low background signal. However, for the red FL, a larger sp2 domain of the π-conjugated carbon is needed, which is not easy to achieve from molecular precursors. In order to fulfill the conditions of this technique, elaborated structures and the chemical composition of carbon precursors are required. So far, only limited carbon materials are known to exhibit the two-photon FL bioimaging performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fruit wastes-derived CDs as bioimaging probes
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the structural and optical features of fruit waste-derived CDs are discussed. A discussion on the bioimaging applications of CDs is also appended toward the end of this section. In Table 2 , the structural, optical, and bioimaging parameters of fruit waste-derived CDs are summarized.

Atchudan et al. synthesized banana peels-derived spherical-shaped CDs [36]. Fig. 4 a shows an HRTEM image of the CDs exhibiting good crystallinity with 0.21 nm lattice spacing. XRD studies confirmed the presence of (002) and (100) planes in CDs [78]. RAMAN spectra revealed a defect ratio of ∼0.7 in CDs corresponding to the D band (1360 cm−1) and G band (1592 cm−1) [79]. The CDs were functionalized with –OH, N–H, CO, CC, (C)3–N, C–OH, C–O–C, HO–CO, and CH3CO functional groups, which imparted dispersibility and hydrophilicity to CDs. Hydrophilic nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized using dwarf banana peels [35]. The CDs were composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements. Nitrogen-doped CDs exhibited graphitic nature along with some traces of amorphous carbon as the CDs were endowed with functional groups, e.g., –COOH, –NH2, –OH, C–N, C–O–C, and CC. As shown in Fig. 4b, watermelon peels-derived CDs were monodispersed, spherical, and amorphous [51]. The main constituents of CDs were carbon (64.65 wt%), hydrogen (7.67 wt%), nitrogen (1.13 wt%) and oxygen (26.55 wt%) elements. Chatzimitakos et al. reported dispersed, amorphous, and spherical CDs synthesized using citrus fruit peels [50]. Both the orange and lemon peels-derived CDs exhibited interlayer spacing closer to graphite. The CDs were functionalized with –OH, N–H, CO, C–H, C–O, –COO, NC(H), and amide/amide (N–C(H)) groups. Another study reported the formation of well-dispersed CDs using orange peels [34]. The lattice spacing of CDs was lesser than graphite, and they were functionalized with CO and –OH groups.

Lychee exocarp-derived CDs were spherical shaped and exhibited a graphitic nature with no definite structure [52]. The CDs were composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements. The surface-passivation treatment of CDs with PEG imparted several functionalities to them. It also improved nitrogen content dramatically from 1.93% to 16.67 wt%. PEG-modified CDs were functionalized with CO, C–OH, N–O, –COOH, CO/CN, and C–O/C–N functional groups. Apple seeds-derived CDs were surface modified with O–H, N–H, C–H, CC, NH2, C–O, C–O–H, and N–H/C–N groups [55]. It added to their photostability and hydrophilicity. Lychee seeds-derived CDs were spherical shaped and amorphous [56]. The main constituents of CDs were carbon (65.58 wt%), hydrogen (3.28 wt%), nitrogen (1.70 wt%), and oxygen (29.44 wt%) elements. Many functional groups such as O–H/N–H, C–H, CO, and C–O were attached to the surface of CDs. XPS analysis also confirmed the presence of C–O, C–N, and CO bonds in CDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, pineapple peels-derived CDs are crystalline with interplanar spacing near to (102) facet of graphite [40]. FTIR spectra confirmed the presence of –OH, CC, and C–O–C surface groups (Fig. 4d), and XPS analysis revealed CC/C–C, C–OH/C–O–C, O–CO, CO, and C–OH bonds in CDs (Fig. 4e). Kiwi fruit peels-derived CDs were spherical shaped and surface modified with –CN, –COOH, and –OH functional groups [42]. The increased interlayer spacing in CDs as compared to graphite was owned to their surface modifications.

Optical characteristics are the most important to decide the bioimaging potential of CDs. Fig. 4f shows UV–visible spectra of dwarf banana peels-based hydrophilic nitrogen-doped CDs [35]. Two absorbance bands were observed at 272 and 320 nm attributing to π-π∗ transitions of aromatic CC (sp2) and n-π∗ transitions of CO/CN bonds, respectively. The visible absorption band is due to the presence of oxygen and nitrogen groups in CDs [80]. Fig. 4g shows the FL excitation spectrum of nitrogen-doped CDs. The maximum excitation wavelength was detected at 345 nm. A high intensity of the excitation peak confirmed a bright FL in CDs. As shown in Fig. 4h, the FL emission was also observed at different excitation wavelengths. With an increase in excitation wavelength, the FL intensity increased too. However, with furthermore increase in the excitation wavelength, a decrease in FL intensity was noted. The CDs were photostable for 0–120 min of excitation. Another report on the banana peels-derived CDs also confirmed two absorbance bands at 277 and 322 nm in the UV–visible spectra [36]. A redshift in FL emission was observed upon increasing the excitation wavelength. The photostability of CDs was tested with the fluorometric method. As shown in Fig. 4i, in daylight, the aqueous solution of CDs was yellowish, but it emitted cyan/blue FL after light irradiation. The CDs were photostable for 90 min. Zhou et al. observed a wide absorption band in the PL spectra of watermelon peels-derived CDs [51]. With an increase in excitation wavelength, the emission band maximum shifted to a longer wavelength, which is due to the inhomogeneity in particle size and distribution of emissive sites in CDs. The FL lifetime of CDs was ∼5.72 ns. The CDs exhibited stability in high salt concentrations and a blue FL upon UV light irradiation.

The citrus peels (orange and lemon)-derived CDs exhibited an absorption band at ∼270 nm owing to the n-π∗ and π-π∗ transition of carboxyl groups and aromatic sp2 domains, respectively [50]. Orange peels-derived CDs exhibited a strong emission band at 455 nm, and lemon peels-derived CDs exhibited two emission peaks at 390 and 435 nm. The FL intensity of orange peels-derived CDs decreased dramatically in acidic or alkaline conditions; however, lemon peels-based CDs showed invariable emission. Gudimella et al. reported two UV–visible absorption peaks at 280 and 350 nm for orange peels-derived CDs [58]. However, Surendran et al. reported absorption bands at 235 and 332 nm for the orange peels-derived CDs [34]. The FL intensity of the CDs increased with an increase in the emission wavelength. The maximum excitation wavelength was observed at 360 nm for the kiwi fruit peels-derived CDs [42]. It has been reported that the particle size, heteroatom doping, and surface modification of CDs are key reasons for the bright FL. Xue et al. studied the optical features of lychee exocarp-derived pristine and functionalized CDs [52]. The PEG-modified CDs exhibited a higher FL emission as compared to the pristine CDs. The FL emission features of CDs-PEG-Ce6-Tf nanoprobe were also investigated in this study. Here, Tf was the navigation molecule, Ce6 (chlorin e6) was the photosensitizer, and CDs were the light-emitting source for bioimaging. The maximum emission wavelength for nanoprobe was 665 nm. Also, the emission wavelength was independent of the excitation wavelength. The nanoprobe was photostable under UV light irradiation for 0.5 h. Putro et al. [57] reported that the surface-passivation of cassava peels-derived CDs controls their PL and not the particle size of CDs. The PL spectra were redshifted upon the addition of PEG to the CDs. With furthermore addition of PEG, the blueshift was observed. A longer electron decay time was noted for PEG-passivated CDs, which confirmed the presence of additional energy levels between valance and conduction bands due to the presence of carboxylic and oxidation functional groups in CDs.

Apple seeds-derived CDs exhibited two characteristic peaks at 270 nm and 340 nm in UV–visible spectra [55]. The PL spectra of CDs also exhibited dual peaks at 380 nm and 415 nm. This behavior is due to the formation of CO2 upon pyrolysis of apple seeds. It gives rise to the carbogenic cores, and the cores co-exist with molecular fluorophores to give FL in the CDs. The PL intensity of CDs was affected by pH variations, and it could be the dissociation of ionizable groups (e.g., CO) in the basic medium. The CDs were photostable in solutions of varying ionic strengths (NaCl or Na2SO4); however, with increased concentration of humic acid, a ∼10% reduction in the PL intensity of apple seeds-derived CDs was observed. Xue et al. observed a wide absorption band in FL emission spectra of lychee seeds-derived CDs [56]. It exhibited a strong emission peak at 440 nm for the excitation at 365 nm. The average FL lifetime of CDs was ∼3.09 ns. The CDs were highly photostable in the salts with varying ionic strengths. Pineapple peels-derived CDs exhibited an absorption peak at 280 nm in the UV–visible spectra [40]. For the excitation-dependent PL behavior, this peak was red-shifted. The CDs exhibited stable FL intensity in high salt concentrations. This particular behavior of CDs was due to the protonation and deprotonation of surface oxygenized groups under acidic and basic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging applications of fruit waste-derived CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Atchudan et al. tested the biocompatibility of dwarf banana peels-derived nitrogen-doped CDs with Clone 9 hepatocytes cells using an MTT assay [35]. The CDs exhibited trivial toxicity to the cells, and with a change in concentration from 0 to 200 μg mL−1, their relative cell viability decreased only to 98%. After 36 h of exposure, a negligible change in the cell viability was noted. The localization of CDs was examined with the FL microscopy images. Fig. 5 a shows the incubation of CDs with Clone 9 hepatocytes for 12, 24, and 36 h. The red, blue, and green FL was observed for the CDs; however, no emission was observed for the control sample. In the cytoplasm and nucleus regions of cells, both feeble and strong emissions were observed during 12 h of the incubation period. In contrast to this, a strong FL emission was observed after prolonged incubation time. The CDs penetrated the cells within 12 h due to their small size. An increase in the FL intensity with the incubation time confirmed the intracellular uptake of nitrogen-doped CDs. In another study, the cytotoxicity of banana peels-derived CDs was tested with nematodes using an MTT assay [36]. Only a small decrease in cell viability of nematodes was noted even at higher concentrations of CDs. This observation confirmed the biocompatibility of banana peels-derived CDs.

The cytotoxicity of orange and lemon peels-derived CDs was tested using the HeLa cell line, and notably, the cell viability of CDs was >90% and 95% after 48 h of incubation, respectively [50]. The orange peels-based CDs were employed for intracellular detection of Fe3+ metal ions in HeLa cells. Due to the small size of CDs, they easily permeate through the cell membrane via passive endocytosis. The CDs emitted weak intracellular FL, which confirmed their uptake in the cytoplasmic region of living cells. In another study, orange peels-derived CDs were surface modified with folic acid to make them biocompatible with normal and cancer cells [58]. Folic acid is important for cell functions, and it also exists in high concentrations in cancer cells [81]. A successful conjugation of folic acid with CDs was confirmed by the UV–visible and FL analysis. The FL of CDs was not affected after conjugation with folic acid. The cytotoxicity of CDs was tested in mouse fibroblast cells (L929 cells) using an MTT assay. The CDs were less toxic with ∼95% cell viability. Both the bioprobes (CDs and CDs-folic acid) were introduced to the MCF-7 cancer cells and irradiated with bright light, UV light, blue light, and green light. No noticeable emission was seen from the control sample, whereas multicolor FL emission was observed from the MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs. A bright FL was observed from the MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs-folic acid. The cellular uptake of the cancer cells improved upon folic acid conjugation of the CDs.

The cytotoxicity of lychee seeds-derived CDs was tested in living cells using the MTT assay [56]. The CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity in living cells. As shown in Fig. 5b, the cell viability of CDs was >90% for a wide range of concentrations. In Fig. 5c, the FL images of HepG2 cells are shown after their incubation with CDs. A blue FL was observed from the intracellular regions at 405 nm. This indicated that the CDs were capable of labeling the cytoplasm and cell membrane regions of HepG2 cells. Apple seeds-derived CDs also exhibited excellent biocompatibility and higher cell viability [55]. The biocompatibility of pineapple peels-derived CDs was tested toward the HeLa and MCF-7 cells using an MTT assay [40]. After 24 h of incubation period, the cell viability of the CDs was ∼92%, and it was over 84% at higher concentrations. The CDs were suitable for intracellular Hg2+ detection.

Zhou et al. reported the excellent photostability and hydrophilicity of watermelon-derived CDs [51]. The HeLa cells were incubated with the CDs at 37 °C. The observance of bright FL in the cell cytoplasm region confirmed the suitability of CDs for bioimaging. Atchudan et al. verified the effects of the concentration of kiwi fruit peels-derived CDs on the cell viabilities of normal and cancerous human cells using a CCK8 assay [42]. Four cell lines (Cal62, MDA-MB-231, BM-MSCs, and BHP10-3SCp) were tested for varying concentrations of kiwi fruit peels-derived CDs. At 24 h, no change in the cell viability of the BM-MSCs cell line was observed as compared to the control sample; however, a decrease in the cell viability was observed after 48 h (Fig. 6 a (i)). The assay revealed no changes in the cell viabilities of the MDA-MB-231 and Cal62 cell lines at 24 h for all the concentrations tested, as shown in Fig. 6a (ii, iii); however, a decrease in the cell viability was observed at 48 h. The cell viability of the BHP10-3SCp cell line was stable at 24 h and 48 h for all the concentrations (Fig. 6a (iv)). As per this analysis, kiwi fruit peels-derived CDs were non-toxic to normal and cancerous human cells up to 150 μg mL−1. So, the CDs are suitable cell labeling agents for the bioimaging of breast and thyroid cancer cells.

Xue et al. examined the biocompatibility of lychee exocarp-derived (CDs-PEG-Ce6-Tf) nanoprobe for the photodynamic therapy in cancer treatment [52,82]. The cytotoxicity of the designed nanoprobe and free Ce6 was checked with the HL-7702 and Bel-7404 cell lines using an MTT assay. With an increase in the concentration of the free Ce6, the cell viability for the Bel-7404 cells decreased to 81%. However, with an increased concentration of the CDs-PEG-Ce6-Tf nanoprobe, it remained intact. It indicated that the nanoprobe exhibited less cytotoxicity and gave rise to lesser cell damage. So, it was apt for live-cell imaging.

The nanoprobe induced 78.5% apoptosis in Bel-7404 cells. Also, the Bel-7404 cells exhibited a higher FL intensity than the HL-7702 cells, which proved that the nanoprobe could effectively enhance the cellular uptake of Ce6 for the cancer cells. The availability of more Tf receptors on the surface of Bel-7404 cells as compared to the HL-7702 cells could be the reason behind its higher FL intensity. Fig. 6b shows the FL images of lychee exocarp-derived nanoprobe for the bioimaging of tumor cells in mice. The imaging was carried out by injecting CDs-PEG-Ce6-Tf subcutaneously into mice. Upon light irradiation, FL was observed immediately from the tumor cells, and it was detectable for ∼120 h. It proved the suitability of the designed nanoprobe for live cellular bioimaging applications.

In conclusion, most of the fruit waste-derived CDs are spherical, amorphous, and enriched with carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen functional groups. This is obvious as fruit waste is a rich source of carbohydrates, proteins, dietary fibers, minerals, and other bioactive compounds. The multiple functional groups impart excellent hydrophilicity and dispersibility to CDs, which, in turn, is beneficial for their FL emission and bioimaging applications. More discussion on the FL mechanisms and related factors for the fruit waste-derived CDs is given in the next section.

As discussed in section 4, fruit waste-derived CDs are suitable for cellular imaging. Some major parameters related to CDs, e.g., hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, toxicity, dispersibility, sensitivity, and photostability, decide their potential for bioimaging [83,84]. So, this section is focused on a critical analysis of these parameters related to fruit waste-derived CDs. It starts with a discussion on the PL mechanisms, the role of chemical compositions, biocompatibility, and hydrophilicity of CDs. Other physicochemical aspects of the fruit waste-derived CDs, such as fruit waste composition, FL emission, synthesis, particle size, crystallinity, dopants, surface-passivation, defects, etc., are also analyzed.

Cayuela et al. suggested three types of CDs as per their lattice structure and the quantum confinement that are amorphous, carbon, and graphene quantum dots [85]. The amorphous CDs are quasi-spherical lacking quantum confinement. The carbon quantum dots are crystalline and exhibit quantum confinement. The graphene quantum dots are the π-conjugated graphene sheets. The synthesis of a particular type of CDs depends on the reaction precursors and synthesis conditions. One of the most attractive features of CDs is their tuneable emissivity covering the blue to NIR regions [6,86–90]. This feature of CDs has opened up the way for many applications; however, the origin of PL in CDs is not clearly understood to date. The three most accepted explanations for the origin of PL in CDs are the surface states, quantum confinement effect, and molecular fluorophores [91,92]. Fig. 7 a shows different FL emission mechanisms in biomass-derived CDs.

The surface states are related to the surface functionalities and the degree of oxidation in CDs. It is further closely associated with the defects in CDs. The higher the number of defects, the better the surface oxidation of CDs. The surface defects may be purity or impurity defects. The purity defects are topological and are related to the oxygen and nitrogen groups in CDs. The oxygen and nitrogen defects are active surface groups, whereas topological defects are morphological. The impurity defects in CDs lower their QY and quench the FL emission. The defect states in CDs trap the excitons, and the recombination of trapped excitons gives a redshift in the FL emission of CDs [93]. As shown in Fig. 7b, an increased surface oxygen content in CDs gives rise to a reduction in the bandgap of CDs and derives a redshift in PL [94]. Different functionalities such as CO, CN, hydroxyl, and amino groups are closely associated with the FL emission behavior of CDs [95].

It is the most widely accepted explanation for the origin of PL in CDs. At the nanoscale, the electron distributions in a semiconductor crystal are affected by the crystal boundaries. It gives rise to bandgap variations and size-based energy relaxations. It is known as the quantum confinement effect when energy carriers at the nanoscale follow scaling laws as per their size variations. The particle size variations in CDs give rise to red-shifted PL behavior [96]. With an increase in the size of CDs, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital shifts to a lower energy level, and the highest occupied molecular orbital shifts to higher energy levels. So, it decreases the bandgap. The electron-donating groups increase the energy levels of CDs; however, the electron-withdrawing groups decrease their energy levels [80].

The FL impurities (molecular fluorophores) are introduced in CDs during their synthesis, and they significantly contribute to the PL behavior of CDs [14,97–99]. The fluorophores may be doped or attached to the surface of CDs. The formation of impurities in CDs is mainly temperature-controlled. At higher temperatures, more fluorophores are generated, giving rise to higher QY in CDs. However, this mechanism is not widely accepted as compared to the other two.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between the chemical composition and FL characteristics of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: This correlation has been a debatable issue among the scientific community as the optical properties of CDs depend on their particle size [6], as well as chemical composition [7]. Several theoretical and experimental studies have supported size-controlled tuneable emission in CDs [100–103]. However, as discussed in section 5.1, the chemical composition of CDs plays a major role in modulating their optical features [104]. Sun et al. synthesized fluorescent CDs that exhibited excitation-dependent FL emission through the entire visible region [7]. The surface energy trap states were emissive in the functionalized CDs, while pristine CDs exhibited dangling bonds and trapped the electrons via the non-radiative pathway. The presence of functional groups like C–O, CO, C–O–C, and OC–OH remove the surface dangling bonds in CDs and assist toward the radiative FL emission. Zheng et al. reported that the FL property of CDs enhanced after their reduction treatment with NaBH4 [105]. This treatment reduces the epoxy and carbonyl groups to hydroxyl groups without affecting the –COOH and CC bonds. The hydroxyl group is electron-donating, and also the removal of carboxyl and epoxy groups via reduction eliminates the non-radiative relaxation paths, so the FL emission of CDs is improved. Bao et al. reported that the degree of surface oxidation controls the FL of CDs [106]. With an increase in the oxidation rate, the oxygen to carbon ratio is improved and a redshift of FL is observed. Li et al. tuned the FL emission of CDs by amine modification of their surface [107]. Interestingly, the FL emission was excitation-dependent for a lower degree of amine functional groups on the surface of CDs, and it was excitation-independent for an amine-rich surface of CDs. Hu et al. achieved tuneable emission of CDs in the entire visible region simply by their oxidation and reduction treatments [108]. The analysis concluded that the nitrogen groups favor blue FL emission and the oxygen groups favor red FL emission in CDs. The ratio of nitrogen to oxygen groups controls the FL emission in CDs. Tang et al. reported that the deep red FL in CDs originates from the graphitic sp2 conjugated core and the blue FL emission comes from the surface states [109]. Wen et al. too confirmed this behavior of CDs [110]. It was reported that the π-conjugated sp2 graphitic structure of CDs governs the excitation wavelength and the surface groups affect the emission behavior of CDs. The surface states act as charge carrier traps in CDs, and fluorescent emission from the states is mostly red-shifted [111]. A strong luminescence in CDs was seen after their treatment with NaBH4 [112]. Jiang et al. also supported the fact that surface oxidation and carboxylation of CDs are responsible for redshift in their FL emission [113]. It was concluded that the particle size was not a major factor in controlling the FL emission of CDs. Another study reported that the surface functional groups not only impart oxidation to CDs but also serve as chemical scissors to break chain groups into smaller-sized CDs, which leads to blueshift in the FL emission [114].

Heteroatom dopants also play an important role in the luminescence of CDs [97]. Zhang et al. tuned the FL emission spectra of CDs from the blue to the yellow region by modulating the nitrogen content [115]. The introduction of nitrogen atoms into CDs imparts excessive free electrons to them. It modulates the electronic structure and improves the optical properties of CDs. Yang et al. introduced sulfur atoms to CDs and observed that these dopants introduced new energy levels [116]. Nitrogen-doping can even control the FL of CDs with multicolor emission features [117]. The introduction of dopants enhances the amorphous characteristics of CDs and gives rise to defects-based surface state emissions. The presence of single or co-dopants in CDs introduces energy traps, and an enhanced FL emission in CDs is owned to their conjugated electronic structure [118–120]. The excessive introduction of dopants to CDs may raise concerns over their toxicity issues also. So, careful control of the chemical composition of CDs is crucial to tuning their FL emission behavior for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition holds the key to the biocompatibility of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biological safety of CDs is a prime concern for their biomedical applications. The main constituent of CDs is carbon, which is a non-toxic element. It makes CDs the safest material to be used as bioprobes for bioimaging applications. Various tests are performed to check the biocompatibility of CDs, e.g., cell viability, cellular uptake, intracellular responses, and biodistribution of CDs [121].

The chemical composition of CDs plays great role in their biocompatibility. The surface modification of CDs is done via covalent or non-covalent methods. The covalent modification of CDs is achieved with halogenations, oxidations, electrochemical or cycloaddition reactions. Some of the well-explored chemical groups are the carboxylic (-COOH) and hydroxyl (-OH), which are covalently attached to CDs [122]. The groups act as chemical handles for the grafting of various aptamers, polymers, proteins, or carbohydrates onto CDs to enhance their sensitivity and selectivity. The carbodiimide chemistry is a covalent approach to attaching biological, organic, or inorganic materials terminated with carboxyl or amine groups to the surface of CDs grafted with amino or carboxyl groups. The covalent modifications of CDs are advantageous, but these treatments may also cause damage to the microstructure and related properties of CDs. As carbon is the core element of CDs, it holds the potential to interact with other biomolecules or polymers via non-covalent interactions, e.g., π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals, or electrostatic interactions. This approach is interesting as it controls the biocompatibility and toxicity issues of CDs while causing minimum damage to their physical or chemical features. Liu et al. designed novel CDs-based bioprobes for the targeted imaging of cancer cells [123]. The hydrogen-bonding interactions between folic acid and carboxyl groups played a major role in the biocompatibility and sensitivity of CDs. In another study, cationic quaternized CDs were grafted with anionic graphene oxide sheets via electrostatic interactions [124]. This non-covalent approach gave rise to biocompatible CDs for the selective labeling of cytoplasm and cell nucleus. The functionalized or bare CDs are non-toxic or least toxic at low concentrations as they are easier to degrade by the excretory system [125]. However, the CDs may cause organ impairment or cell toxicity at higher concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilicity of CDs for the bioimaging applications
ce:section.ce:para: For the biological applications, not only the fluorescent properties of CDs are important, but they should also be able to exhibit the vitality of organisms or host cells. The organo-solubility of CDs is a crucial factor in deciding their bioimaging potential. Some common hydrophilic groups, e.g., carbonyl, hydroxyl, amino or carboxyl are immobilized to CDs in order to enhance their hydrophilicity. The solubility of CDs improves their dispersion in aqueous mediums. It increases the quantum confinement of CDs, which otherwise is lost if they are agglomerated. It improves the QY and FL emission of CDs [126,127]. It is advantageous to synthesize CDs using fruit waste as it is rich in several biomolecules that inherently provide multiple dopants and functional groups for improved organo-solubility of CDs [128].

Top-down and bottom-up are the two well-used methodologies to synthesize the CDs [129,130]. A classification of these methodologies is given in Table 3 . Biomass-derived CDs are mostly synthesized via bottom-up methods such as pyrolysis, HTC, template-assisted, solvothermal, microwave-assisted methods, etc. Some waste materials (tire soot, candle soot, or natural gas soot) are broken down into CDs following top-down methods also [131,132]. The bottom-up synthesis of CDs undergoes four steps that are condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [133–136]. In the first step, organic molecules form chain-like intermediates via condensation, i.e., amidation, aldol condensation, Schiff base condensation, or radical reactions. The reaction immediates further get polymerized to generate polymer-like CDs following covalent or non-covalent interactions. In the third step, polymer CDs form a carbon core at high temperatures. In the final stage, the surface modifications of CDs take place with the residual reactants or surface-passivation agents. However, a better clarity of underlying mechanisms, formation, polymerization, reorganization processes, and reactions between different functional groups in CDs is still needed for the bottom-up synthesis.

Most of the fruit waste, such as watermelon peels [51], citrus fruit peels [50,58], lychee exocarp [56], casava fruit peels [57], lychee seeds [56], and apple seeds [55], is processed via pyrolysis to synthesize CDs (Fig. 8 a). On the other hand, banana [35,36], pineapple [40], and orange [34] peels are processed via the HTC technique. The choice of synthesis method affects the particle size distribution and QY of CDs. As per the analysis given in Table 2, the HTC method is more effective for converting fruit waste into CDs. The fruit waste treated via HTC exhibited higher QY as compared to others treated via pyrolysis. The orange peels-derived CDs treated via the HTC method exhibited smaller particle size [34] as compared to the orange peels treated via pyrolysis [50]. Hydrothermally treated cassava peels-derived CDs [137] exhibited different FL emission as compared to CDs treated via the microwave-assisted method [57]. It shows the importance of selecting a particular synthesis approach to control the structural and optical characteristics of CDs. Also, as per the advantages of HTC, pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted methods (Table 3), a higher QY, uniform size distributions, and tuneable doping of CDs are achievable with these approaches. This indeed has been observed for fruit waste-derived CDs (Table 2); however, fruit waste composition plays an important role in deciding the particular behavior of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical analysis of fruit waste-derived CDs
Fruits waste-constituents, conversion, and fluorescent emission
ce:section.ce:para: Most parts of fruits contain sugars, including starch, maltose, cellulose, and glucose [138]. During the carbonization process, sucrose is hydrolyzed to form glucose and fructose. At high temperatures, long chains of cellulose get disrupted and transform into oligomer compounds and glucose. The products undergo isomerization, dehydration, and decomposition to generate ketones, aldehydes, organic acids, and furfural derivatives. They are further converted into polymer products following the dehydration, polymerization, and condensation reactions. In the next step, the aromatic clusters are formed upon aldol condensation and cycloaddition of polymer products. At the supersaturation point of concentration, a burst of nucleation is generated, and finally, the CDs are produced. The hydrophilic and oxygen-containing fragments in the solution form a passive layer surrounding the CDs, which forms the functionalities endowed hydrophilic shell and the hydrophobic core [139]. The CO functional group is present in all the sugar components in fruit waste, so the n-π∗ transition mode is mostly observed in the FL spectra of CDs, as discussed in Section 4.2. Two transition modes, i.e., n-π∗ and π-π∗ are observed in the FL spectra of fruit waste-derived CDs. As the rate constant is smaller for the π-π∗ transition to cross the bandgap to the triplet state as compared to the n-π∗ transition, and so π-π∗ transition gives a stronger FL. Fruit waste contains benzene that enhances the conjugated degree of the system. It assists in an easier realization of the π-π∗ transition and so the FL emission in fruit waste-derived CDs [140].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the size, crystallinity, and synthesis conditions on the FL emission
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of CDs controls their energy levels and the PL. The larger size of CDs makes them more sensitive toward the light at a higher wavelength, whereas the smaller size of CDs makes them more sensitive toward the light at a shorter wavelength. This trend is well observed for the fruit waste-derived CDs (Fig. 8b). Banana peels [36], dwarf banana peels [35], and citrus fruit peels-derived CDs [50,58] exhibited large diameters and higher excitation wavelengths. It is to be noted here that the banana peels and dwarf banana peels have been given the same synthesis treatment, but they give rise to different structural and optical features owing to their raw compositions. The fruit waste-derived CDs having smaller diameter sizes, viz., watermelon peels [51], apple seeds [55], and orange peels [34], exhibited lower excitation wavelengths. This behavior is due to the narrow size distribution in CDs, which gave rise to a higher FL emission. The crystalline nature of CDs with sp2 conjugated graphitic structure gives rise to a higher QY and the FL emission in CDs. As shown in Fig. 8c, banana peels [36], dwarf banana peels [35], and pineapple peels-derived CDs [40] are crystalline in nature and exhibit a higher QY. On the other hand, watermelon peels [51], orange peels [34], and lychee seeds-derived CDs [56] exhibited poor crystallinity and lower QY. The apple seeds-derived CDs show an exceptional behavior as they are amorphous in nature but still have a higher QY [55]. The reason behind it could be the presence of multiple surface functional groups in apple seeds-derived CDs like O–H, N–H, C–H, CC, NH2, C–O, C–O–H, N–H, and C–N [141].

The reaction temperature controls the extent of carbonization in CDs (Fig. 8d). Fruit waste has been mostly treated at 150–180 °C. To be more specific, seeds of fruits are treated at higher temperatures as compared to peels (Table 2). Pineapple and banana peels-derived CDs, which have been treated at 150 °C and 200 °C, respectively, exhibited a higher QY [35,36,40]. Apple and lychee seeds-derived CDs, which have been treated at 300 °C, exhibited a lower QY, [55,56]. Watermelon peels-based CDs exhibited the least QY among all the fruit waste-derived CDs [51]. The pineapple and banana peels-derived CDs exhibited crystalline structures and mono-dispersity [35,36,40]. However, the watermelon peels, apple, and lychee seeds-derived CDs are amorphous in nature. This behavior clarifies that raw composition and temperature are the controlling factors to decide the graphitic or non-graphitic nature of CDs. The reaction temperature is also closely related to reaction time; the longer the reaction time, the higher the degree of carbonization in CDs. It promotes the growth of graphitic core in CDs, which affects their FL emission. Banana and kiwi fruit peels are treated for 24 h, which assists in achieving a higher QY and crystallinity in CDs [35,36,42]. Other fruit wastes are mostly treated for 1–2 h [40,50–52,55,56]. However, the pineapple peels-based CDs were treated for 2 h and exhibited a maximum QY among all the fruit waste-derived CDs [40]. It again signifies that the material composition and reaction conditions play important roles in deciding the structural and optical characteristics of CDs. So, it is difficult to derive the generalized behavior of the CDs. This, in fact, is true for the other natural materials-derived CDs also. A comparison of the synthesis parameters and QY of fruit waste-derived CDs with other natural materials-derived CDs is given in Table 4 .

The PL of CDs is due to photogenerated electron-hole pairs that induce radiative recombination of trapped carriers localized in the sp2 bonded carbon architectures surrounded by sp3 defects. This process remains active in the presence of dopants also, and it functions parallelly with other emission modes [142]. Being compositionally rich, fruit waste-derived CDs inherently contain a variety of heteroatom dopants, e.g., nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, phosphorous, etc. Nitrogen is the most adaptable dopant in CDs as its atomic size is comparable to carbon. The nitrogen doping in CDs creates new surface states and modulates their electronic structure [143]. As compared to nitrogen, phosphorous atoms are larger in size than carbon atoms. The phosphorous atoms in CDs give substitutional defects and act as the n-type donor. Similarly, the sulfur doping alters the electronic structure of CDs [140]. The dopants introduce new energy levels in the π and π∗ orbitals of CDs; the electron transitions and interband crossings further branch out in CDs to give multiple peaks in the FL emission spectra [144,145]. The rich composition of fruit waste led to multiple functionalities and dopants in the CDs. A higher QY of dwarf banana peels-derived CDs is related to the presence of higher nitrogen content in it as compared to the other fruit waste-derived CDs [35].

Different edges, like zigzag, oxygen, or amine groups-based, are possible in fruit waste-derived CDs. The dangling bonds and surface groups in CDs control their optical characteristics. The photoexcited electrons in CDs, which are relaxed from the sp2 energy levels, give rise to the blue-emission; however, the surface states in CDs give rise to the red-emission [108,146]. The oxygen functional groups in CDs create defects on basal or edge planes of CDs and give a longer wavelength emission. The bandgap in the CDs decreases with an increased number of the oxygen functional groups. The carboxyl groups also narrow down the bandgap in CDs as they give rise to significant defects and local distortions in sp2 conjugated hybridization [147]. The electron-donating groups enhance the FL emission, but the electron-withdrawing groups quench the FL. The electron-donating groups increase the π-electron cloud density giving rise to enabled electron-hole recombination centers, while electron-withdrawing groups lower the π-electron density and enable the non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers in CDs [80]. The oxygen groups in CDs exhibit pH-dependent FL as the protonation, deprotonation, electrostatic charging, or doping of oxygen gives rise to the Fermi level shift of the electronic band structure in CDs [148]. Fruit waste-derived CDs are surface modified with carbon and oxygen groups, and so FL is owned to the radiative recombination of excited electrons from the n-π∗ transitions of CO groups. The blue FL originates from π-π∗ transitions of core states and the green FL is owned to n-π∗ transitions of the edge states [149].

The surface passivation is a functionalization approach to enhance the optical properties of CDs. It decreases the surface defects in CDs and improves the exciton-hole recombination probability. It prevents the agglomeration of CDs and increases the FL intensity. It is achieved in two ways, by oxidizing the surface of CDs with strong acids or by capping the CDs with long-chain agents. Interestingly, the fruit waste-derived CDs are self-passivated because of the presence of long-chain carboxyl acid and other functional groups in the fruit waste itself [6]. The surface passivation of lychee exocarp-derived CDs with PEG changed their elemental composition from carbon: 49.81 wt%, oxygen: 48.26 wt%, nitrogen: 1.93 wt% to carbon: 39.58 wt%, oxygen: 43.75 wt%, nitrogen: 16.67 wt% [52]. The cassava peels-derived CDs were also surface passivated with PEG [57]. Interestingly, not only the size of CDs but the extent of surface passivation has also been found to control their optical features. In PL spectra, a redshift in the wavelength was noted after the addition of PEG to CDs. With further addition of PEG, a blueshift in the PL spectra of CDs was observed.

CDs have emerged as the most versatile and valuable carbon nanomaterials. In almost two decades of research on CDs, a vast amount of literature has been published on optical imaging, surface modifications, and fabrication approaches to make them promising for environmental, energy, and biological applications. CDs have gained much attention due to three basic reasons, i.e., they are easy to synthesize using various economic carbon precursors, surface modification of CDs is easier, and they have unique physicochemical properties. In the past few years, there has been a new trend to convert the environmental waste into CDs. It is both an environment-friendly and financially viable strategy to synthesize CDs that are self-passivated and biocompatible.

Fruit waste is the third largest contributor to the environmental pollution. Although it is a waste it holds large nutritional value. The conventional fruit waste management techniques need high operational costs, generate low-value products, or cause serious air pollution issues, which need to be replaced with novel, more efficient and productive solutions to treat the fruit waste. The modern conversion of fruit waste into CDs following simpler synthesis treatments is a highly efficient and economical approach. It is the perfect remedy for handling environmental waste, while simultaneously generating value-based products for advanced applications. It can turn on several business opportunities in the future if large-scale synthesis (i.e., quantity and reproducibility) of fruit waste-derived CDs turns into reality. It seems very interesting, but the actual realization of this environmental waste for real-world applications has not been achieved yet. So, concerted efforts are required to treat fruit waste in the most critical manner to unfold its underlying potential. The present review article discussed the progress of fruit waste-derived CDs for bioimaging applications with an emphasis on the synthesis methods and physicochemical and optical properties of CDs. The composition of fruit waste plays a major role in deciding the morphological and FL emission features of the CDs. The choice of fruit waste (peels or seeds), synthesis methods, reaction parameters (time or temperature), heteroatom dopants, and surface modifications of CDs are controlling parameters to modulate their electronic structure, which gives rise to the surface and defects energy states to achieve tuneable FL emission in CDs. The fruit waste-derived CDs are suitable for bioimaging applications owing to their less toxicity, high solubility, excellent biocompatibility, as well as photostability.

Although fruit waste is perfect for synthesizing biocompatible CDs; the composition of fruit waste is, however, diverse, depending upon the origin and choice; hence, it affects the consistency, reproducibility, and product yield of the final product. Furthermore, it requires characterizations and comprehensive studies of raw precursors to achieve the desired quantity and quality of the CDs. Fruit waste is largely available, but still, no full-scale or pilot methods for the large-scale synthesis of CDs have been reported so far. It will be needed for the futuristic scale-up of CDs for industrial applications. There can be traces of metallic or non-metallic dopants in CDs during the synthesis process due to the compositional diversity of fruit waste, which needs to be purified to avoid any optical modulations in CDs. Also, widely recognized PL mechanisms are needed for the practical applicability of CDs. The surface functional groups in CDs decide their solubility in different solvents; however, precise control over the surface functionalities during the synthesis of CDs is not achieved yet. More importantly, CDs with desired selectivity for the analytes of interest need to be designed to avoid the FL quenching. For the biomedical applications of fruit waste-derived CDs, it is important to synthesize CDs with the desired level of tuning for the higher intracellular photostability in order to achieve persistent bioimaging. Novel CDs with absorption wavelength in the far-red to NIR regions are also required as the absorption wavelength of CDs in the UV to blue regions limits their bioimaging potential. A high wavelength emission of CDs is urgent for deep tissue penetration in tissues.

Fruits are a major part of the horticultural production worldwide. Our planet houses many species of fruits, but so far, only few of them have been explored for synthesizing CDs. More efforts are required for the conversion of different fruit waste into CDs. It will be helpful also to derive better generalizations on the behavior of fruit waste for a successful implementation of their bioimaging and other applications. Most importantly, more studies on the real-time iv vivo bioimaging applications of fruit waste-derived CDs are encouraged for their actual clinical applications. Novel utilization of hybrid fruit waste to synthesize CDs is also suggested to blend the compositional features of different fruit waste for versatile surface modifications and dopants in CDs. Another possibility could be to use some chemicals along with the fruit waste to achieve sharp optical features in the CDs. Also, future efforts are required for the long-lasting PL and afterglow emission in fruit waste-derived CDs. Hopefully, the fruit waste-derived CDs will meet their actual potential soon to serve the public health and other fields of interest.
